trust_assumption: A GPS-disciplined oscillator or PTP grandmaster trusts the GNSS-derived time-of-day and 1PPS edge it recovers from the satellite signal as an unforgeable physical reference, so every downstream consumer (synchrophasor PMUs, 5G RAN frame timing, financial timestamping, data-center NTP roots, sequence-of-events recorders) treats the disciplined clock as authoritative absolute time and never cross-checks the rate at which that time is allowed to move.
violation: An attacker spoofs the GNSS RF to slew the recovered time — not the position — by tens of microseconds to milliseconds, walking the disciplined clock past the holdover/insanity thresholds slowly enough that the grandmaster never declares loss-of-lock, so PMU phase-angle math, 5G slot boundaries, MiFID/transaction sequencing, and consensus quorum windows all silently act on a falsified 'now' that every component still labels as GPS-authoritative.

techniques.name: Slow time-slew GNSS spoof under loss-of-lock threshold
Disciplined-clock holdover-bypass via spoofed 1PPS edge

rationale: Public GNSS-spoofing tooling and most detection research target the position fix; the timing dimension is the under-modeled seam because in 2025-2027 power grids, 5G fronthaul, financial venues, and data-center fabrics increasingly depend on GPS-disciplined time as their single authoritative clock with no monotonic-rate sanity check independent of the GNSS source. The trust violated is that recovered time can only move forward at real-world rate; a spoofer who slews under the receiver's own holdover/insanity thresholds keeps the lock indicator green while corrupting the absolute reference, so synchrophasor protection, slot scheduling, and timestamp ordering act on falsified time that telemetry still attests as good. This is distinct from generic GNSS position spoofing and from NTP/PTP transport attacks: the disciplined-clock-as-ground-truth assumption is the seam, no product reconciles GNSS-derived time-rate against an independent holdover oscillator's drift envelope, and ATT&CK ICS has no technique for timing-reference falsification.
suggested_research: Build a multi-source time-sanity monitor that bounds GNSS-disciplined time against a disciplined free-running oscillator's predicted drift envelope and flags any slew exceeding physical plausibility before loss-of-lock fires; red-team a PTP grandmaster with an SDR slow-slew profile and measure how far PMU/5G consumers can be walked while the lock LED stays green.

trust_assumption: An autonomous platform's navigation/guidance stack (drone flight controller, ag/mining autonomy, maritime ECDIS autopilot, last-mile robot) trusts the GNSS fix as the absolute ground-truth anchor that corrects its inertial dead-reckoning, so a plausibly-continuous position stream is fused into the state estimator and converted directly into actuation (waypoint, heading, geofence, return-to-home) without the fix ever competing against an independent absolute reference.
violation: An attacker injects a coherent spoofed GNSS constellation that smoothly captures the receiver and then drags the reported position along an attacker-chosen trajectory consistent with vehicle dynamics, so the autonomy treats spoofed data as control input — steering the platform, triggering a false geofence breach, or commanding return-to-home toward attacker coordinates — because the state estimator weights the externally-supplied fix as truth over its own physics.

techniques.name: Coherent capture-and-drag GNSS trajectory injection
Spoofed-fix overriding INS fusion as actuation input

rationale: GNSS position spoofing is demonstrated in the literature, but the frontier seam for 2025-2027 is the data->control coupling inside increasingly autonomous platforms where the fix is not merely displayed to a human but fed directly into a closed-loop estimator that actuates. The trust violated is that an externally-radiated position is ground truth that should dominate the platform's own inertial physics; a dynamics-consistent capture-and-drag defeats the cheap consistency checks (jump detection, signal-strength) most COTS receivers ship. This differs from manual-navigation spoofing because the victim is an automated control loop with machine-speed reaction and no human sanity check, and AI-driven trajectory synthesis makes the drag arbitrarily plausible. No COTS autonomy stack cross-validates GNSS against independent absolute references (vision/terrain/RF-ranging) as an authority arbiter rather than a smoother, and there is no detection that distinguishes a physics-consistent slow drag from real motion.
suggested_research: Prototype an authority-arbitration layer that demotes GNSS from ground truth to one vote among vision/terrain-matching/multi-antenna-bearing and refuses actuation when the fix diverges from the inertial-only prediction beyond a dynamics bound; demonstrate capture-and-drag against an open-source flight controller and measure the divergence before any onboard alarm.

trust_assumption: A satellite (especially COTS-bus SmallSat/CubeSat or a legacy bird) trusts that a telecommand arriving on the correct uplink frequency, in the expected CCSDS/AX.25 frame format, and within the ground-station contact window originated from its authorized operator, so the onboard command handler executes the command on the basis of channel and format conformance rather than strong per-command cryptographic authentication.
violation: An attacker with SDR transmit capability and orbital/contact-window knowledge transmits a well-formed telecommand on the legitimate uplink during a pass, and the spacecraft's command handler — lacking authenticated command counters or rejecting only on framing errors — accepts and executes attacker commands (mode change, attitude maneuver, payload toggle, memory write) as if from the operator, because possession of the format and frequency is treated as possession of authority.

techniques.name: Unauthenticated CCSDS telecommand injection on live uplink
Command-window format conformance accepted as operator identity

rationale: The 2025-2027 explosion of cheap COTS-bus SmallSats, ride-share constellations, and amateur/academic missions has multiplied birds that ship with weak or absent telecommand authentication, where the implicit access-control model is 'right frequency + right frame + our contact window = us.' The trust violated is identity->authority: channel and format conformance is mistaken for operator identity, an exact P2 confused-deputy where the deputy is the spacecraft's own command handler. This is a live frontier because ground-segment SDR is commodity and orbital ephemerides are public, yet there is essentially no public tooling and no on-orbit detection that an executed command lacked cryptographic provenance. It is distinct from ground-network compromise: the seam is the radio command-acceptance policy itself, which CCSDS SDLS adoption has not yet closed across the COTS fleet.
suggested_research: Survey COTS flight-software command stacks (e.g., open-source cFS-derived handlers) for whether authenticated command counters/SDLS are enforced versus framing-only acceptance, and build a ground-side telecommand provenance verifier that refuses to relay any command lacking a fresh authenticated counter; model the SDR cost and pass-window window for an unauthenticated injection.

trust_assumption: A SmallSat integrator trusts that each COTS subsystem it bolts onto the spacecraft bus (reaction wheels, GNSS receiver, radio, OBC, star tracker) ships authentic vendor firmware and speaks the bus (CAN/SpaceWire/I2C/MIL-STD-1553) as a benign peer, so any board that enumerates correctly on the bus inherits implicit trust to source telemetry and accept commands without inter-component authentication.
violation: A subverted or counterfeit COTS component — firmware tampered in the distribution channel or a clone board sold into the rapid-integration market — enumerates normally and then, once on orbit, abuses its bus peer position to forge telemetry, drop or alter commands to sibling subsystems, or trigger at a chosen time, because the bus grants every enumerated board the same trust the integrator extended to the vendor's brand.

techniques.name: Counterfeit COTS subsystem forging intra-bus telemetry
Tampered subsystem firmware as trusted bus peer

rationale: New-space economics push integrators toward commodity, rapidly-sourced subsystems and tight schedules, so spacecraft buses increasingly look like unauthenticated peer fabrics where 'enumerates correctly' equals 'is the genuine vendor part.' The trust violated is provenance: the receiver checks the board's bus identity label, not the authenticity of its firmware or origin, and intra-bus protocols (1553/CAN/SpaceWire) typically lack component attestation. This is a frontier with no public tooling and no on-orbit detection because hardware attestation on COTS smallsat buses is essentially nonexistent and a malicious peer's forged telemetry is indistinguishable from a faulty sensor. It is distinct from ground supply-chain attacks on flight software: the seam is the on-bus inter-subsystem trust that survives launch, where a single tampered board becomes a persistent insider with physical authority over attitude or payload.
suggested_research: Define a lightweight intra-bus component-attestation scheme (per-subsystem signed boot measurement gossiped on 1553/SpaceWire) and a ground-side telemetry-provenance cross-check that correlates a subsystem's reported state against independent sibling sensors to flag a lying peer; build a hardware-in-the-loop testbed injecting a counterfeit reaction-wheel controller.

trust_assumption: A Vessel Traffic Service, port-management system, collision-avoidance display, and maritime-domain-awareness analytics platform trusts that an AIS message carrying an MMSI, position, course, and identity was broadcast by the real vessel it names, so it ingests AIS as authoritative real-time ground truth for traffic deconfliction, automated routing, and sanctions/anomaly analytics without any cryptographic origin binding.
violation: An attacker transmits or replays unauthenticated AIS frames over VHF (or injects via a compromised AIS aggregator/feed) to fabricate phantom vessels, clone a real MMSI to a decoy track, or erase a ship's presence, and the VTS/MDA stack treats the spoofed broadcasts as genuine positions — corrupting collision-avoidance, triggering false port/safety responses, or laundering a vessel's true movements past sanctions analytics.

techniques.name: Unauthenticated AIS phantom-vessel and MMSI-clone injection
AIS aggregator feed poisoning into MDA analytics

rationale: AIS has no authentication by design and 2025-2027 maritime operations increasingly automate on AIS feeds (autonomous-assist collision avoidance, sanctions and dark-fleet analytics, port digital twins), so the broadcast-as-truth assumption now feeds machine decisions, not just a watchstander's screen. The trust violated is provenance: the consumer believes an MMSI identifies the vessel that sent it, when any SDR can forge frames and aggregator APIs can be poisoned upstream. This is an emerging seam rather than mature classic spoofing because the novel exposure is the analytics/automation layer that fuses AIS as ground truth, and AI-driven track synthesis makes phantom fleets statistically plausible. Detection is only nascent (kinematic/RF-fingerprint anomaly research), there is no cryptographic AIS origin binding deployed at scale, and ATT&CK does not model maritime broadcast trust.
suggested_research: Build a cross-source AIS validator that fuses terrestrial-VHF receive-fingerprint, satellite-AIS, and radar/EO tracks to score each MMSI track's physical plausibility and flag clones/phantoms, and red-team an MDA analytics pipeline by injecting dynamics-consistent dark-fleet tracks to measure false-clearance of sanctions screens.

trust_assumption: An air-traffic surveillance fusion system, UTM/drone-traffic service, and increasingly an aircraft's own ADS-B-In/TCAS display trusts that an ADS-B broadcast carrying an ICAO address and GNSS-derived position came from the aircraft it identifies, so it fuses the squitter into the surveillance picture and conflict-detection logic as a real cooperative target without origin authentication.
violation: An attacker transmits unauthenticated 1090ES/UAT ADS-B squitters to inject ghost aircraft, spoof an ICAO identity onto a false track, or shift a reported position, and the surveillance fusion or UTM deconfliction logic treats the fabricated targets as genuine cooperative traffic — generating spurious conflict alerts, distorting the drone-traffic picture, or masking a real intruder by saturating the airspace model.

techniques.name: Ghost-aircraft ADS-B squitter injection into fusion
ICAO-identity spoof onto fabricated ADS-B track

rationale: ADS-B carries no authentication and the 2025-2027 frontier is its expansion into UAS Traffic Management and automated detect-and-avoid, where ghost targets feed deconfliction algorithms that command drone maneuvers rather than merely informing a controller. The trust violated is provenance: a GNSS-sourced position relayed over an open datalink is accepted as the named aircraft's true state. This is emerging rather than purely classic because the consumer shifting from human controllers (who apply multilateration cross-checks) to automated UTM logic that may trust the broadcast directly is the new exposure, compounded by GNSS-spoofing feeding falsified positions into otherwise-genuine transponders. Multilateration/RF-fingerprint detection is only partially deployed, there is no authenticated ADS-B at scale, and UTM-layer trust of cooperative broadcasts is unmodeled.
suggested_research: Develop a UTM-side cross-validator that requires multilateration or multi-receiver time-difference-of-arrival agreement before a cooperative ADS-B target influences automated deconfliction, and quantify how injected ghost tracks alter detect-and-avoid maneuver commands in an open UTM simulator.

trust_assumption: A PTP/NTP time client trusts that the network path to its time source is symmetric — that forward and reverse propagation delays are equal — so it computes its clock offset as half the round-trip and applies the result as authoritative time correction, having no way to independently measure one-way delay and therefore no way to detect that the symmetry assumption was violated.
violation: An on-path attacker (transparent-clock relay, compromised switch, or queue-manipulating middlebox) introduces a controlled asymmetric delay — adding latency to only one direction — which the client folds into its offset calculation as a real time error and slews its clock by the injected asymmetry, so the consumer's notion of 'now' is shifted by an amount it cannot detect because authenticated PTP/NTP packets protect content but not path symmetry.

techniques.name: Directional delay injection skewing PTP offset
Transparent-clock asymmetry exploiting half-RTT assumption

rationale: As enterprises move off GNSS toward network-delivered PTP/NTP for resilient timing (telco fronthaul, exchanges, data-center fabrics), the under-modeled seam is that even fully authenticated PTP (with security TLVs) and NTS-secured NTP defend message integrity but cannot defend the half-round-trip symmetry assumption baked into offset math. The trust violated is temporal: the client believes its computed offset reflects real clock error when an attacker has injected directional delay it cannot observe. This is distinct from the GNSS-time gap (no satellite involved) and from spoofing/replay (packets are genuine and authenticated): the attacker manipulates only path timing. There is no deployed detection because one-way delay is not independently measurable on a single path, and SIEMs do not model per-direction latency asymmetry as a time-integrity signal.
suggested_research: Prototype multi-path/redundant-source asymmetry detection that cross-checks offset estimates from independent network paths or a disciplined holdover and flags divergence consistent with one-directional delay injection; demonstrate a controlled-asymmetry attack against a PTP-secured grandmaster and measure the achievable undetected clock skew.

trust_assumption: Distributed-consensus and certificate-validation logic trusts the local clock as an honest absolute time source when it enforces time-bounded rules — block/timestamp acceptance windows, validator slot timing, TOTP/OCSP/JWT freshness, and certificate notBefore/notAfter validity — so each node independently decides validity against its own clock without treating that clock as an attacker-influenceable input shared across the quorum.
violation: An attacker who shifts the time source feeding a set of nodes (via the GNSS or PTP/NTP gaps above, or NTP manipulation) moves their certificate validity windows and consensus timing simultaneously, so revoked or not-yet-valid certificates are accepted, expired tokens pass freshness checks, or a coordinated clock skew across enough validators reshapes which blocks/slots are considered timely — turning a falsified shared 'now' into an authorization and liveness weapon.

techniques.name: Coordinated clock skew widening cert validity acceptance
Consensus slot-timing manipulation via shared time-source skew

rationale: Security logic everywhere assumes the local clock is a trustworthy oracle: certificate validity windows, OCSP/CRL freshness, JWT exp, TOTP, and proof-of-stake slot/attestation timing all gate authorization or liveness on time the node never treats as attacker-controllable. The frontier seam for 2025-2027 is that the same nodes increasingly share a small number of upstream time sources (GNSS-disciplined or NTP/PTP), so a single time manipulation correlates across the quorum and across the PKI simultaneously — a revoked cert re-accepted on a skewed node, or enough validators skewed to bias slot timeliness. The trust violated is that 'now' is an honest local fact rather than a shared, spoofable input. This is distinct from the clock-source gaps it builds on: the novel seam is the consensus/PKI layer's unexamined assumption that time is non-adversarial, with no tooling that treats clock provenance as part of the validity decision.
suggested_research: Model how far an attacker must skew N validators or relying parties to flip a quorum-timeliness or cert-validity decision, and prototype a validity layer that attaches signed time-source provenance/uncertainty to each notBefore/notAfter and slot check so a node refuses time-gated decisions when its clock confidence degrades.

trust_assumption: A distributed-energy-resource fleet (rooftop solar inverters, home batteries, EV chargers, smart thermostats) trusts that a setpoint or curtail/dispatch command arriving from its cloud aggregator or VPP/DERMS platform over IEEE 2030.5/OpenADR is a legitimate utility instruction, so each device actuates real power output on the aggregator's say-so, and the grid operator in turn trusts the aggregator's reported fleet capacity as dispatchable reserve.
violation: An attacker who compromises the aggregator/VPP cloud, its API tokens, or the command channel issues a synchronized command to thousands of geographically clustered DER devices — a simultaneous trip, ramp, or curtail — and the inverters obey because device-side authority is delegated wholesale to the aggregator, producing a coordinated multi-megawatt step change that the bulk grid never authorized and the operator's reserve model did not anticipate.

techniques.name: Synchronized DER fleet trip via compromised aggregator
VPP capacity-report trust into bulk reserve dispatch

rationale: Aggregated DER and virtual power plants are scaling fast in 2025-2027, with single cloud platforms holding dispatch authority over fleets large enough to matter at transmission scale, yet device-side trust is a wholesale delegation: the inverter authorizes the aggregator's position, not the legitimacy or grid-safety of each command. The trust violated is identity->authority — possession of the aggregator's command channel becomes possession of synchronized control over thousands of grid-edge actuators. This is an emerging seam because the concentration of dispatch authority and the standardized command protocols (2030.5/OpenADR) are recent, and the bulk-grid operator's reserve model trusts aggregator capacity reports it cannot independently verify. There is no tooling to detect an anomalous synchronized DER command and only nascent grid-edge anomaly research; ATT&CK ICS models device control but not the aggregator-as-mass-actuator concentration.
suggested_research: Model the frequency/voltage impact of a synchronized DER step from a single compromised aggregator and design a device-side rate-and-coincidence guard that refuses to honor implausibly-correlated fleet-wide commands, plus an operator-side validator that cross-checks aggregator capacity claims against metered telemetry before counting them as reserve.

trust_assumption: A safety instrumented system, building-automation controller, or medical-device monitoring loop trusts that the sensor/telemetry value it last received over an unsequenced field link (BACnet/IP, Modbus/TCP, medical device telemetry) reflects the current physical state, so its automated safety/control response and the SOC's monitoring both act on the most-recent reading as if it is fresh and live.
violation: An attacker on the network captures a normal-state telemetry frame and replays it (or holds the last good value) while the real process diverges, so the controller's automated response and the operator/SOC view continue to act on a stale value presented as current — suppressing a safety trip, masking a dangerous excursion, or holding a building/medical setpoint while the true measured quantity has moved into an unsafe regime.

techniques.name: Stale-telemetry replay suppressing automated safety response
Last-good-value freeze masking process excursion

rationale: Safety-telemetry field protocols (BACnet, Modbus, many medical-device links) carry no per-message freshness/sequence authentication, and the 2025-2027 frontier is that the consumer of these readings is increasingly an automated safety/response loop and an ML-driven SOC rather than a human watching a gauge, so a replayed or frozen value directly suppresses an automated trip or alarm. The trust violated is temporal freshness: true-at-capture is treated as true-at-use, with no liveness proof binding the reading to the present moment. This is distinct from generic ICS replay because the seam is specifically the freshness contract feeding automated safety decisions and cyber-physical alarm suppression, where the absence of a heartbeat/nonce means stale data is indistinguishable from live. Detection is only nascent (timing/jitter anomaly research) and no field-protocol freshness enforcement is widely deployed.
suggested_research: Design a lightweight freshness/heartbeat overlay (signed monotonic counter or challenge-nonce) for BACnet/Modbus/medical telemetry that lets the consumer prove a reading is live, and build a passive detector that flags telemetry whose value-vs-jitter signature indicates a held or replayed frame; validate against a building-automation and an SIS testbed.
